text: Psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi spent decades studying why people find solitude uncomfortable and discovered something that would have surprised Pascal: the problem is not existential dread but cognitive mismatch. His research showed that unstructured time produces more negative affect than almost any other condition — including work. The mind, when given no task, defaults to rumination. Csikszentmihalyi called this 'psychic entropy': the tendency of awareness to slide toward anxiety and self-criticism unless actively organized. The quiet room is not peaceful by default. It must be made so — and that making is the practice.
question: What did Csikszentmihalyi's research identify as the default state of the mind during unstructured time?

options.text: A gentle drift toward creative insight and spontaneous problem-solving
Psychic entropy — a slide toward anxiety and self-criticism
A neutral baseline of low arousal that is neither pleasant nor unpleasant
Existential dread rooted in awareness of one's own mortality
